task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (`template_str: str`) and return `list[str]` of logical IDs
- The function must raise `TypeError` with a message that explicitly names both `None` and the actual received type (e.g., `"Expected str, got NoneType"`) when the input is `None` or any non-string type; the check must use `isinstance(template_str, str)` and treat `None` as a distinct case mentioned in the message
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a message that includes the phrase "Invalid JSON" and the original error detail
- The function must validate that the result of `json.loads` is a `dict`; if it is any other type (e.g., a JSON array, string, or scalar), raise a `ValueError` with a message that states the template must be a JSON object and names the actual type received
- The function must validate that the parsed dict contains a top-level `Resources` key; raise a `ValueError` with a message containing the word "Resources" and indicating the key is missing if absent
- The function must validate that the value of `Resources` is a `dict`; raise a `ValueError` with a message that names the actual type received if it is any other type (e.g., a list or `None`)
- The function must return a list of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as the logical IDs; an empty `Resources` dict must return an empty list without raising any exception
- The function must include a docstring that describes: (1) the expected input as a JSON-encoded CloudFormation template string, (2) the return value as a list of logical ID strings, (3) all raised exceptions (`TypeError` and `ValueError`) with their exact triggering conditions, and (4) at least one usage example in doctest format (e.g., `>>> extract_logical_ids('{"Resources": {"MyBucket": {}}}')`) demonstrating a valid call
- Each individual value within the `Resources` dict must be validated as a `dict`; if any resource entry is not a `dict` (e.g., it is a string, list, or `None`), raise a `ValueError` with a message identifying the offending logical ID and the actual type received

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, otherwise return False:
- Use exactly one regex pattern variable; it must be referenced directly in a `re.fullmatch` or `re.match`/`re.search` call — never defined but unused
- The regex must be a single unified pattern with no alternation (`|`) at the top level; do not split logic into two or more alternating branches (e.g., no `pattern_a|pattern_b` structure)
- Length must be enforced by exactly one mechanism: embed `{3,63}` as the sole top-level quantifier in the regex OR use a single explicit `len()` check — never both; a regex using `+`, `*`, `{1,61}`, `{2,63}`, or any quantifier that permits fewer than 3 or more than 63 characters at the top level is invalid
- The recommended single unified pattern is of the form `^(?!.*--)[a-z0-9]([a-z0-9-]{1,61}[a-z0-9])?$` combined with a `len()` guard, OR a fully self-contained pattern like `^(?!.*--)[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]{1,61}[a-z0-9]$` that inherently enforces 3–63 chars without any separate `len()` check — choose one approach and apply it consistently
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are not permitted
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit (not a hyphen)
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens (e.g., 'my--bucket' is invalid); enforce this with a single negative lookahead `(?!.*--)` inside the unified pattern
- Must not be formatted as an IPv4 address; since dots are already excluded by the character rule, document this in the docstring rather than adding a separate regex check
- Every variable defined in the function must be used; no dead code or unused bindings
- Do not include redundant validation logic: if the regex enforces a constraint, do not also enforce it with a separate conditional; each rule must be checked in exactly one place
- The function must include a docstring listing each enforced rule and explicitly stating that the function returns False (never raises an exception) for any violation

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `aws`, `aws-us-gov`, and `aws-cn`, e.g. `arn:aws:iam::`, `arn:aws-us-gov:iam::`, and `arn:aws-cn:iam::`
- The regex must use **named capture groups** `(?P<account_id>...)` and `(?P<role_name>...)` for the account ID and role name respectively
- The account ID capture group must match exactly 12 digits
- The role name capture group must match valid IAM role name characters (alphanumeric, `+`, `=`, `,`, `.`, `@`, `_`, `-`) with length 1–64; path prefixes (e.g. `role/path/rolename`) must NOT match
- The `match_arn` function must guard against `None`, empty string, and whitespace-only string inputs using an explicit check `if arn is None or arn == '' or arn.strip() == ''` and return `None` in all three cases without raising an exception
- Include a docstring on the `match_arn` function that explains each named capture group and the overall pattern structure
- Include an assertion confirming that a role name of exactly 64 valid characters matches, and a separate assertion confirming that a role name of 65 characters does NOT match, directly testing the upper bound of the length constraint
- Include an assertion with a role name consisting solely of all valid special characters (`+=,.@_-`) to confirm the full character class is accepted
- Include at least 3 valid ARN examples (covering different partitions) and 3 invalid ARN examples (e.g. wrong format, non-12-digit account, missing role) with assertions confirming correct match or non-match behavior and that named capture groups return the expected account ID and role name values
- Include assertions confirming that a whitespace-only string (e.g. `'   '`) and an ARN containing a path prefix in the role segment (e.g. `arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/path/rolename`) each return `None`

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string; call json.loads() explicitly on string input before any further processing
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or str type, checked before any parsing or flattening
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a JSON string input cannot be parsed by json.loads()
- If the root value after parsing is not a dict, return a flat dictionary with a single empty-string key mapped to the scalar value, making this edge case explicit and intentional
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac'); list indices must be converted to strings explicitly using str(i) before concatenation, not implicitly via f-string formatting
- Include a recursion depth guard with a max_depth parameter defaulting to 20; raise a ValueError when depth >= max_depth (not depth > max_depth), so recursion never exceeds exactly max_depth levels
- The internal recursive helper must be called with depth=0 on the root dict, and the docstring must explicitly state that depth=0 represents the first level of keys, depth=1 the second, and so on, and that a ValueError is raised when depth reaches max_depth (i.e., at the 21st level when max_depth=20)
- Include a complete docstring with: (1) parameter descriptions including max_depth with its default and exact semantics, (2) return type, (3) all raised exceptions, (4) an inline example using EC2 metadata that explicitly demonstrates the depth-limit behavior by showing a call with a small max_depth value and the resulting ValueError, in addition to a normal flattening example
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value using standard Python typing conventions

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must use a capture group (parentheses) that wraps the entire key pattern so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent
- The pattern must match all common AWS key ID prefixes: AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA (not just AKIA)
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters (A-Z, 0-9), for a total key length of 20 characters
- The pattern must include word boundary assertions (\b) or equivalent non-word-character anchors on both sides of the full key to prevent false positives from partial matches within longer strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' must not match); the trailing \b is required even though [A-Z0-9]{16} ends on a word character, to make the boundary intent explicit and documented
- The regex must be compiled or invoked with re.ASCII (re.A) flag explicitly set to enforce that \b and character classes operate on ASCII characters only, preventing any ambiguity around Unicode word boundaries or case folding
- The solution must not use re.IGNORECASE (re.I) under any circumstances; this must be enforced AND explicitly documented in the code via an inline comment (e.g., '# re.IGNORECASE intentionally omitted') or a runtime assertion (e.g., assert not (compiled_pattern.flags & re.IGNORECASE)) placed immediately adjacent to the regex compilation or invocation
- The function must accept a string input and return a list of all non-overlapping matched key IDs found in that string using findall or equivalent, so that log parsing across multi-key inputs is supported
- The function must validate that the input parameter is of type str and raise a TypeError with a descriptive message (e.g., 'Input must be a string') if a non-string value is passed, preventing AttributeError from propagating
- The function must include a docstring that describes: (1) the purpose of the function, (2) the accepted AWS key prefixes, (3) the expected input type and return type, and (4) an explicit note that case-insensitive matching is intentionally disabled for security correctness; additionally, the regex pattern itself must have an inline comment explaining each major component (prefix alternation, character class, word boundaries)

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring describing the parameter, return value, any exceptions raised, and at least one runnable `doctest` example demonstrating correct usage
- Explicitly reject `bool` values before the `int` check by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message (e.g., `'timestamp_ms must be an int, not bool'`), since `bool` is a subclass of `int` and would otherwise pass `isinstance` validation silently
- Validate the input: raise `TypeError` if the value is not an `int` (and not a `bool`), and raise `ValueError` if the value is negative or exceeds the maximum allowable millisecond timestamp for year 9999 (i.e., `253402300799999`), with an inline comment on that constant explaining its derivation (e.g., `# datetime.max as Unix ms: 9999-12-31 23:59:59 UTC`)
- Handle `None` explicitly by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message before any other validation or conversion logic
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds by dividing by `1000` before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp` with `tz=timezone.utc` (never `utcfromtimestamp`, which is timezone-naive and deprecated); the `tz=timezone.utc` argument is mandatory to guarantee timezone-independent results across all execution environments
- Define `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS = 253402300799999` as a module-level constant (not inside the function body), with an inline comment explaining its derivation (e.g., `# datetime.max as Unix ms: 9999-12-31 23:59:59 UTC`), so it is reusable and clearly scoped
- Format the output using `strftime` with the explicit format string `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"` to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes; verify any doctest example values by independently confirming the UTC conversion (e.g., `1623760200000 ms / 1000 = 1623760200 s = 2021-06-15T12:30:00Z`) so the expected output is provably correct
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars)
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings and must include BOTH current runtimes (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17) AND legacy/deprecated runtimes still accepted by AWS (e.g., nodejs14.x, python3.7, python3.8, java11) — omitting legacy runtimes will not be accepted
- Handler must include a pattern constraint (e.g., ^[^\s]+$ or similar) and a maxLength of 128 to reflect AWS limits
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240)
- Environment Variables keys must be strictly enforced using a propertyNames constraint with pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ so that ALL keys in the Variables object are validated — relying solely on patternProperties without propertyNames is insufficient and will not be accepted
- The Environment Variables Variables object must use propertyNames to enforce key naming — if patternProperties is also present, it must not contradict propertyNames and its inclusion should be justified; using both with identical patterns is considered redundant and should be avoided in favor of propertyNames alone
- The Environment Variables object must enforce a maximum of 128 key-value pairs via maxProperties to reflect AWS limits
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900
- MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240
- The schema must declare "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#" and must not use draft-2019-09 or later keywords such as unevaluatedProperties that are unsupported in draft-07
- Every top-level property and every nested object property must include a "description" annotation string explaining its purpose and any AWS-specific constraints (e.g., limits, formats, or valid values); schemas lacking description fields will not be accepted
- The schema itself must include a top-level "title" field (e.g., "AWS Lambda Function Configuration") and a top-level "description" field summarizing the schema's purpose

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of item dictionaries in DynamoDB boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared as number), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- For `N` type comparisons, both the stored value (a numeric string) and the condition value must be converted to `float` before comparison using a single expression (e.g., `float(stored) == float(condition_value)`), without branching on the type of `condition_value`; the docstring must explicitly state that `condition_value` for `N` types may be either a number or a numeric string
- Input validation must check `attribute_name` in this exact order: (1) raise `ValueError` if it is not a `str` instance (which also covers `None`), then (2) raise `ValueError` if it is an empty string; no redundant `is None` check separate from the `isinstance` check is permitted
- The function must validate inputs and raise a `ValueError` if the items list is `None` or contains non-dict elements, and silently skip items that do not contain the specified attribute
- The internal type extraction helper must raise a `ValueError` when it encounters an unsupported DynamoDB type key, rather than returning `None` or silently ignoring it, to prevent accidental matches against `None`
- All type hints must use `typing` module constructs compatible with Python 3.8+ (e.g., `List[Dict[str, Any]]` from `typing` rather than the built-in `list[dict]`), and the docstring must note the Python version compatibility
- The function must include full type hints on all parameters and the return value
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, and raised exceptions, explicitly documenting: the `ValueError` for invalid `attribute_name`, the `ValueError` for unsupported DynamoDB types, and the behavior of `condition_value` for `N` type comparisons (accepting both numeric and numeric-string values)

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions in three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1), using a flexible pattern that does not hardcode exact character counts for any segment.
- The pattern must capture the region code from all common AWS endpoint URL formats: standard service endpoints (service.region.amazonaws.com), S3 path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), S3 virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com), and endpoints with additional subdomains (sub.service.region.amazonaws.com).
- The region must be captured in a named capture group (e.g., (?P<region>...)) so it can be extracted directly without relying on fixed positional groups or string splitting.
- The region pattern must account for the full known structure of AWS region codes: one or more lowercase letter segments for the geographic area and direction (allowing for either one or two letter-only dash-separated segments before the numeric suffix), followed by a single numeric identifier segment (e.g., -1, -2), without hardcoding exact character counts and without requiring more letter segments than necessary.
- The regex must NOT use variable-width lookbehinds; Python's re module requires fixed-width lookbehinds, so the solution must use only fixed-width lookbehinds, non-capturing groups, or explicit structural anchoring instead.
- The region capture must be anchored to the .amazonaws.com suffix — specifically, the regex must require that the named region group is immediately followed by \.amazonaws\.com (with an optional path or end of string), ensuring the match is structurally tied to a valid AWS endpoint and not a lookalike substring.
- The region must be preceded by a literal dot (.) in the regex — the pattern must use a fixed-width lookbehind (?<=\.) or consume and discard the dot via a non-capturing group, but must NOT use (?:^|\.) as an anchor, since this allows matching at the start of a string without a preceding dot and could produce false positives on non-URL strings.
- When the dot preceding the region is consumed (not matched via lookbehind), the solution must document that only the named capture group (?P<region>) should be used for extraction, not the full match string, to avoid inadvertently including the leading dot in the extracted value.
- The regex must not produce false positives by capturing non-region tokens such as 'amazonaws', 's3', or bucket name subdomains; the pattern must structurally distinguish the region segment from preceding service or subdomain tokens by requiring the region to be the last dot-separated token before .amazonaws.com.
- The solution must be a single regular expression string, usable directly with Python's re module (re.search or re.findall) without importing any third-party regex module, and must correctly extract only the region portion from a provided test URL string.

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters; raise `ValueError` for negative `size_bytes` or unrecognized `storage_class` values
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Include a clearly commented pricing constants block at the top of the function with a note indicating the date these values were last verified against AWS public pricing, so values can be audited and updated
- Define all size tier boundaries using **decimal terabytes**: 1 TB = 1,000,000,000,000 bytes (10^12), 1 GB = 1,000,000,000 bytes (10^9), 1 KB = 1,000 bytes — never use binary (1024-based) conversions for AWS billing thresholds or size floors
- Apply AWS tiered pricing for `STANDARD`: $0.023/GB for first 50 TB (50×10^12 bytes), $0.022/GB for next 450 TB, $0.021/GB beyond 500 TB, using decimal TB boundaries as defined above
- For `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128 KB (128,000 bytes, decimal): compute cost as `max(size_bytes, 128_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128 KB (128,000 bytes, decimal) and note the 90-day minimum storage duration in a comment; compute one month's cost as `max(size_bytes, 128_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `GLACIER`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 40 KB data plus 8 KB metadata overhead (floor = 48,000 bytes, decimal); note the 90-day minimum duration in a comment; compute one month's prorated cost as `max(size_bytes + 8_000, 48_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 40 KB (40,000 bytes, decimal); note the 180-day minimum duration in a comment; compute one month's prorated cost as `max(size_bytes, 40_000) / 1e9 * rate_per_GB`
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128 KB (128,000 bytes, decimal) matching AWS practice; apply the frequent access tier rate ($0.023/GB) to `max(size_bytes, 128_000)`; include a dedicated comment block explicitly stating: (1) 100% frequent access tier is assumed, (2) the 128 KB minimum billable size is enforced per AWS practice, and (3) the monitoring fee represents exactly one object's share of the $0.0025 per 1,000 objects per month fee, i.e., $0.0000025 per object — do not use $0.0025 as a flat per-object fee
- Return the total estimated monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
